text: Last class: we should weigh the options, and we had better decide today.
Today: in Houston meetings start on time, whereas in Brazil we warm up first.

US meetings start on time, whereas Brazilian meetings warm up first.
Americans value directness, while Brazilians often build rapport first.
Unlike a traditional company, this firm has a flat hierarchy.
Their humor is similar to ours, but personal space is different from ours.
Maria Claudia, welcome to Houston. One tip: our meetings value punctuality and directness. How does that compare to your office?
You start right on the agenda, whereas we usually build rapport first. Our custom is a little small talk before business.
That is good to know. And our hierarchy is flat -- unlike a traditional firm, a junior here may openly disagree with me.
Interesting. Your formality in the first email is different from ours -- informal in tone, while we open more formally. I will adjust my mindset.
Honestly, our taste in art is similar to yours. A comment on the museum district is the best gesture to turn an acquaintance into a partner.
Perfect. So I will keep my warm custom of building rapport, but respect your etiquette -- on time, direct, and ready to talk about art.
US meetings start on time, whereas ours warm up first.
Unlike a traditional firm, their hierarchy is flat.
Their taste in art is similar to ours.
Their email etiquette is different from ours.
I keep my custom of building rapport, and I respect their etiquette.

Maria Claudia, before your visit to Houston, let me share a few notes on how we work here, because the customs are a little different from Brazil. First, punctuality. In the US, meetings tend to start exactly on time, whereas in Brazil there is often a softer start with some warm-up. Here, if a call is at nine, we begin at nine. Second, directness. Americans are usually quite direct; we say what we think early in the meeting, while in Brazil people often build the relationship first and approach the hard point more gently. Neither way is better; they are simply different. Third, hierarchy. Our teams are fairly flat, so a junior analyst may challenge a director openly, unlike some traditional Brazilian companies where the hierarchy is stronger. Fourth, small talk. We do keep it short, but we value it, especially around art and sports, so a comment on the museum district will build rapport fast. And finally, etiquette in email and gesture. We are informal in tone but precise in content. My advice is simple: keep your warm Brazilian mindset, add a little American punctuality and directness, and you will fit in perfectly.

Before I fly to Houston, I want to think clearly about culture, because the way I work in Brazil is not always the way my partners work in the US. When I compare the two, I want my language to be precise. I will use whereas and while to contrast: in Brazil meetings tend to open with warm conversation, whereas in the US they start right on the agenda. I will use unlike to mark a clear difference: unlike my usual style, I will state my main point early. And I will use similar to and different from for the softer comparisons: the American sense of humor is similar to ours, but the sense of personal space is quite different from ours. I do not want to lose my own custom of building rapport; that warmth is a strength. But I will adapt my mindset on punctuality and directness, respect the flatter hierarchy, and watch the small details of etiquette, from the firmness of a handshake to the gesture I use when I disagree. Culture is not about right and wrong. It is about reading the room, comparing fairly, and meeting people where they are.

First, you observe the local custom before you judge it, so you compare cultures fairly and not from your own habits alone. Then, you name one clear difference using whereas or while, for example punctuality or directness. After that, you mark a strong contrast with unlike, when one culture does the opposite of the other. Next, you soften the comparison with similar to and different from, because most differences are partial, not total. Finally, you adapt your own mindset where it helps, while you keep the strengths of your own culture, such as warmth and the habit of building rapport.
